SUMMARY INFORMATION.Contracting Organisation: City St George’s, University of London
SUMMARY INFORMATION.Application Title: Developing equitable, personalised community rehabilitation interventions for people with peripheral artery disease.

Lead Applicant.Organisation: City St George’s, University of London

APPLICATION DETAILS.Plain English Summary:
Why is this research needed?
Out-of-hospital rehabilitation (known as community rehabilitation) improves people’s health and wellbeing and
saves money. But community rehabilitation is not provided equally, and it is not known how best to treat people
with some conditions or whether treatments are acceptable to patients. Improving equal access to high quality
community rehabilitation is central to the government’s NHS plan.
One of the conditions that we don’t know how to rehabilitate in the community is called peripheral artery disease.
Peripheral artery disease is poor leg blood flow due to narrowed arteries. People from Black, African Caribbean,
South Asian or Eastern European communities, those with diabetes or living in areas with socioeconomic
deprivation are affected more by peripheral artery disease.
Severe peripheral artery disease can cause constant leg pain, sores and gangrene in the toes. This is called
chronic limb threatening ischaemia. Most people need their arteries unblocked in hospital and then need to self-
manage their recovery at home. But there are no self-management programmes to support people with chronic
limb threatening ischaemia to recover and live well at home.
Some people with milder peripheral arterial disease experience leg pain on walking (called intermittent
claudication). Walking frequently is the best way to improve this pain and reduce the risk of severe artery disease.
It is hard for people to walk on their own when they are in pain. New ways are needed to help people walk
regularly, improve their leg pain and do more activities.
Many people with peripheral artery disease are not active enough to reap the health benefits. This increases their
risk of getting other health problems. There are many ways to help people to become active. One way is to use a
smartwatch that records how much activity is done and sends messages to encourage people to be more active.
Smartwatches can be used separately or as part of an activity programme. But some older people find
smartwatches difficult to understand and use.
Aims
To work with patients, practitioners and service planners to co-design ways to provide community rehabilitation
equitably for everyone with peripheral artery disease and explore new ways to help people to be more active.
People from groups who are most affected by peripheral artery disease and underrepresented communities will be
included.
What will happen?
I will build a team to
1. summarise the evidence for the factors that influence self-management, interview patients and
practitioners and co-design a self-management programme to help people with chronic limb threatening
ischaemia live well at home.
2. look at the results from a study of an existing walking exercise programme for people with intermittent
claudication and use this information to adapt this programme so it can be provided in the community.
3. interview patients and practitioners, co-design and user test a new treatment including a smartwatch to
help everyone with peripheral artery disease be more active.
4. co-design a plan to implement these treatments in the NHS, if these ways are shown to be effective and
acceptable.
How have patients and communities been involved?
Members of the public and patients from different backgrounds chose this topic and helped design this research.
They helped write this summary. The public advisory group will meet every 6-months and be involved in co-design
and workstream management.
Sharing this research
Patients, practitioners, service planners and voluntary and professionals’ organisations (like Circulation
Foundation) will co-design and help share our research progress and findings, so it is available in the NHS and for
the public. This may include writing research summaries; giving talks; hosting meetings; designing infographics,
animations, websites and social media.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Scientific Abstract:
Provision of community rehabilitation is inequitable. Many interventions are not evidence-based or acceptable to
patients or providers. Rehabilitation of some high burden conditions, such as peripheral artery disease (PAD),
receive relatively little national support, prioritisation or research funding, fuelling health inequalities.
PAD affects over 237 million adults globally. One common PAD symptom is intermittent claudication (IC) although
in its most severe manifestation, it leads to chronic limb threatening ischaemia (CLTI). Over half of those with
symptomatic PAD will die, have an amputation or cardiovascular event within five years of diagnosis. CTLI
prognosis is extremely poor; commensurate with terminal cancer. The economic burden of PAD is substantial and
rising.
PAD patients report dissatisfaction with care and improving outcomes across the PAD symptom range is the
highest ranking James Lind Alliance research priority among all vascular conditions. Providing PAD rehabilitation
and supporting self-management will save money. Yet, equitable access to community rehabilitation is
compromised by lack of acceptable, feasible interventions that were co-designed with patients and providers,
restricted resources and a high and increasing demand from a diverse patient group with complex needs. Many
people with PAD are also excluded from research and unable to access or use new treatments e.g., digital
interventions.
New scalable, co-designed, inclusive PAD interventions such as provider supported community rehabilitation, self-
management programmes and ‘next-generation’ mobile based health technologies (mhealth) are promising. Yet,
evidence is mixed, none have been designed with PAD patients and providers, or commissioned in the NHS.
‑
The aim of this professorship is to transform the care for people with peripheral artery disease through community
rehabilitation interventions. Over five work packages, I will lead research that will:
1. Maximise diverse and inclusive involvement, participation and engagement of PAD underserved groups.
2a. Through systematic review, qualitative and participatory approaches, codesign an inclusive supported self-
management intervention to improve quality of life in CLTI patients.
2b. Develop trial protocol, funding application, set-up/commence a trial.
3a. Through secondary analysis of existing quantitative and qualitative data, participatory approaches, co-refine
and feasibility test an inclusive home-based walking exercise behaviour-change programme for people with IC in
primary and community services.
3b. Develop trial protocol, funding application, set-up/commence a trial.
4a. Through qualitative approaches, co-design, adaptation and user testing, develop an inclusive mhealth
intervention for PAD patients to increase physical activity.
4b. Develop trial protocol and submit funding application.
5. Drawing on data from workstreams 2-4, specify the implementation context, outcomes and mechanisms and co-
design a programme theory, preliminary implementation and evaluation strategies for inclusive PAD community
rehabilitation services.
This ambitious programme of work balances building on my existing work with guaranteed high impact outputs,
and projects which promise a rapid upward trajectory within five years. It will spearhead the shift of PAD care into
the community and develop research capacity in under represented disciplines.
Mitigating research and digital exclusion and involving patients and the public are central to this research. Patients
identified the research questions, helped plan this research and will help deliver and mobilise research progress
and findings using co-designed, creative approaches.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Working with People and Communities Summ:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
The public have framed this professorship so that inclusion is at its core. Regularly, over two years, I discussed my
research with diverse PAD patients and their carers in coproduction meetings that have shaped the focus,
research questions, and plan. Nineteen people with lived experience of PAD, including people from Black, African
Caribbean, South Asian, and Eastern European communities and those with CLTI shared their experiences of
living with PAD and their treatment. To reach people from underserved communities who did not want to join a
group or those experiencing digital exclusion, I talked to them individually via telephone or in-person.
They discussed the barriers to receiving rehabilitation in hospital (e.g. time, travel) and how they wanted to receive
co-ordinated and responsive rehabilitation/support locally. They described how this research was ‘much needed’
as this part of NHS care was ‘unacceptable and burdensome’ and they asked for ‘better access’ to rehabilitation
and ways to help them look after their condition and symptoms independently.
Patients with CLTI described having treatment in hospital and then ‘being left in a vacuum at home’. They
welcomed sessions with a provider to help them ‘make the most of their lives’. People with IC told me that home-
based walking exercise was often ‘overlooked as a treatment’, but that specific walking advice with support was
needed. They liked the content and format of MOSAIC1 and commented that having this intervention provided in
the community with ‘enough time with the therapist’ was key to helping them walk more. They didn’t mind who
treated them ‘just as long as they were well trained and supervised’.
All advisors with lived experience of PAD recognised that becoming more active was important and wanted to
explore mhealth using activity trackers, although some were nervous about this being the only treatment option.
They were worried about ‘everything going online’ and suggested developing an intervention that was easy to use,
inclusive and accessible to everyone. This advice changed the focus of this workstream. The public advisors
helped identify research questions and develop the research plan. Three advisors (two from ethnic communities)
read the public summary.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
My public involvement strategy aligns with the NIHR 2025-2030 strategic commitments and six UK Public
consistency99,100.
Involvement Standards, ensuring valuable, sustainable relationships, quality and
Standards 1-3: Our advisory group includes men, women and members of underrepresented populations (n=9). I
will seek further representation from underserved populations and diverse geographical locations by continuing to
work with patient and community groups. I will employ a dedicated research inclusion and public involvement
officer to plan, support and liaise with our advisors and community groups, co-ordinate our regular meetings and
ad hoc activities. I will be responsible for oversight, planning and reporting of involvement, impact, and diversity in
involvement, participation and engagement. Research inclusion and cultural competency training will be offered to
public advisors.
Standards 4-6: My team will meet with our public advisors every 6-months to discuss workstream progress, and
work with them in between on focused activities. We will balance meaningful involvement with the risk of over-
burdening them. Digital exclusion will be mitigated by providing iPads, data packages and technical support, if
needed, or if a member is not able to join the larger online meeting, they will be sent materials and can speak
individually either remotely or via telephone. Advisors will be offered involvement in elements of workstreams that
particularly interest them and invited to monthly research management meetings. Planned activities include
providing feedback on public facing materials such as websites, reviewing study processes to enhance
acceptability and experience of participation, promoting public awareness of the study, reaching hidden
communities and dissemination. We will fund our public advisors to co-present at conferences and become co-
investigators on funding applications.
Standard 6: Public involvement will be monitored, reviewed and reported at research management meetings, with
public advisors providing feedback, reviewing and agreeing reported impact. This will enable reflection on our
processes to improve advisor experience and explore quality and impact. We will report our public involvement in
research using the Guidance for Reporting Involvement of Patients and the Public 2-short-form checklist79. All
involvement activity and expenses will be reimbursed in line with NIHR policy.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
Not applicable.

SUMMARY BUDGET:
The finance section should provide a breakdown of costs associated with undertaking the research as
described in the proposal.

plain_english_summary.text:
Why is this research needed?
Out-of-hospital rehabilitation (known as community rehabilitation) improves people’s health and wellbeing and
saves money. But community rehabilitation is not provided equally, and it is not known how best to treat people
with some conditions or whether treatments are acceptable to patients. Improving equal access to high quality
community rehabilitation is central to the government’s NHS plan.
One of the conditions that we don’t know how to rehabilitate in the community is called peripheral artery disease.
Peripheral artery disease is poor leg blood flow due to narrowed arteries. People from Black, African Caribbean,
South Asian or Eastern European communities, those with diabetes or living in areas with socioeconomic
deprivation are affected more by peripheral artery disease.
Severe peripheral artery disease can cause constant leg pain, sores and gangrene in the toes. This is called
chronic limb threatening ischaemia. Most people need their arteries unblocked in hospital and then need to self-
manage their recovery at home. But there are no self-management programmes to support people with chronic
limb threatening ischaemia to recover and live well at home.
Some people with milder peripheral arterial disease experience leg pain on walking (called intermittent
claudication). Walking frequently is the best way to improve this pain and reduce the risk of severe artery disease.
It is hard for people to walk on their own when they are in pain. New ways are needed to help people walk
regularly, improve their leg pain and do more activities.
Many people with peripheral artery disease are not active enough to reap the health benefits. This increases their
risk of getting other health problems. There are many ways to help people to become active. One way is to use a
smartwatch that records how much activity is done and sends messages to encourage people to be more active.
Smartwatches can be used separately or as part of an activity programme. But some older people find
smartwatches difficult to understand and use.
Aims
To work with patients, practitioners and service planners to co-design ways to provide community rehabilitation
equitably for everyone with peripheral artery disease and explore new ways to help people to be more active.
People from groups who are most affected by peripheral artery disease and underrepresented communities will be
included.
What will happen?
I will build a team to
1. summarise the evidence for the factors that influence self-management, interview patients and
practitioners and co-design a self-management programme to help people with chronic limb threatening
ischaemia live well at home.
2. look at the results from a study of an existing walking exercise programme for people with intermittent
claudication and use this information to adapt this programme so it can be provided in the community.
3. interview patients and practitioners, co-design and user test a new treatment including a smartwatch to
help everyone with peripheral artery disease be more active.
4. co-design a plan to implement these treatments in the NHS, if these ways are shown to be effective and
acceptable.
How have patients and communities been involved?
Members of the public and patients from different backgrounds chose this topic and helped design this research.
They helped write this summary. The public advisory group will meet every 6-months and be involved in co-design
and workstream management.
Sharing this research
Patients, practitioners, service planners and voluntary and professionals’ organisations (like Circulation
Foundation) will co-design and help share our research progress and findings, so it is available in the NHS and for
the public. This may include writing research summaries; giving talks; hosting meetings; designing infographics,
animations, websites and social media.

scientific_abstract.text:
Provision of community rehabilitation is inequitable. Many interventions are not evidence-based or acceptable to
patients or providers. Rehabilitation of some high burden conditions, such as peripheral artery disease (PAD),
receive relatively little national support, prioritisation or research funding, fuelling health inequalities.
PAD affects over 237 million adults globally. One common PAD symptom is intermittent claudication (IC) although
in its most severe manifestation, it leads to chronic limb threatening ischaemia (CLTI). Over half of those with
symptomatic PAD will die, have an amputation or cardiovascular event within five years of diagnosis. CTLI
prognosis is extremely poor; commensurate with terminal cancer. The economic burden of PAD is substantial and
rising.
PAD patients report dissatisfaction with care and improving outcomes across the PAD symptom range is the
highest ranking James Lind Alliance research priority among all vascular conditions. Providing PAD rehabilitation
and supporting self-management will save money. Yet, equitable access to community rehabilitation is
compromised by lack of acceptable, feasible interventions that were co-designed with patients and providers,
restricted resources and a high and increasing demand from a diverse patient group with complex needs. Many
people with PAD are also excluded from research and unable to access or use new treatments e.g., digital
interventions.
New scalable, co-designed, inclusive PAD interventions such as provider supported community rehabilitation, self-
management programmes and ‘next-generation’ mobile based health technologies (mhealth) are promising. Yet,
evidence is mixed, none have been designed with PAD patients and providers, or commissioned in the NHS.
‑
The aim of this professorship is to transform the care for people with peripheral artery disease through community
rehabilitation interventions. Over five work packages, I will lead research that will:
1. Maximise diverse and inclusive involvement, participation and engagement of PAD underserved groups.
2a. Through systematic review, qualitative and participatory approaches, codesign an inclusive supported self-
management intervention to improve quality of life in CLTI patients.
2b. Develop trial protocol, funding application, set-up/commence a trial.
3a. Through secondary analysis of existing quantitative and qualitative data, participatory approaches, co-refine
and feasibility test an inclusive home-based walking exercise behaviour-change programme for people with IC in
primary and community services.
3b. Develop trial protocol, funding application, set-up/commence a trial.
4a. Through qualitative approaches, co-design, adaptation and user testing, develop an inclusive mhealth
intervention for PAD patients to increase physical activity.
4b. Develop trial protocol and submit funding application.
5. Drawing on data from workstreams 2-4, specify the implementation context, outcomes and mechanisms and co-
design a programme theory, preliminary implementation and evaluation strategies for inclusive PAD community
rehabilitation services.
This ambitious programme of work balances building on my existing work with guaranteed high impact outputs,
and projects which promise a rapid upward trajectory within five years. It will spearhead the shift of PAD care into
the community and develop research capacity in under represented disciplines.
Mitigating research and digital exclusion and involving patients and the public are central to this research. Patients
identified the research questions, helped plan this research and will help deliver and mobilise research progress
and findings using co-designed, creative approaches.

background_and_rationale.text:
Out-of-hospital rehabilitation (known as community rehabilitation) improves people’s health and wellbeing and
saves money. But community rehabilitation is not provided equally, and it is not known how best to treat people
with some conditions or whether treatments are acceptable to patients. Improving equal access to high quality
community rehabilitation is central to the government’s NHS plan.
One of the conditions that we don’t know how to rehabilitate in the community is called peripheral artery disease.
Peripheral artery disease is poor leg blood flow due to narrowed arteries. People from Black, African Caribbean,
South Asian or Eastern European communities, those with diabetes or living in areas with socioeconomic
deprivation are affected more by peripheral artery disease.
Severe peripheral artery disease can cause constant leg pain, sores and gangrene in the toes. This is called
chronic limb threatening ischaemia. Most people need their arteries unblocked in hospital and then need to self-
manage their recovery at home. But there are no self-management programmes to support people with chronic
limb threatening ischaemia to recover and live well at home.
Some people with milder peripheral arterial disease experience leg pain on walking (called intermittent
claudication). Walking frequently is the best way to improve this pain and reduce the risk of severe artery disease.
It is hard for people to walk on their own when they are in pain. New ways are needed to help people walk
regularly, improve their leg pain and do more activities.
Many people with peripheral artery disease are not active enough to reap the health benefits. This increases their
risk of getting other health problems. There are many ways to help people to become active. One way is to use a
smartwatch that records how much activity is done and sends messages to encourage people to be more active.
Smartwatches can be used separately or as part of an activity programme. But some older people find
smartwatches difficult to understand and use.

To work with patients, practitioners and service planners to co-design ways to provide community rehabilitation

People from groups who are most affected by peripheral artery disease and underrepresented communities will be
included.

I will build a team to
1. summarise the evidence for the factors that influence self-management, interview patients and
practitioners and co-design a self-management programme to help people with chronic limb threatening
ischaemia live well at home.
2. look at the results from a study of an existing walking exercise programme for people with intermittent

3. interview patients and practitioners, co-design and user test a new treatment including a smartwatch to

the public. This may include writing research summaries; giving talks; hosting meetings; designing infographics,
animations, websites and social media.
Provision of community rehabilitation is inequitable. Many interventions are not evidence-based or acceptable to
patients or providers. Rehabilitation of some high burden conditions, such as peripheral artery disease (PAD),
receive relatively little national support, prioritisation or research funding, fuelling health inequalities.
PAD affects over 237 million adults globally. One common PAD symptom is intermittent claudication (IC) although
in its most severe manifestation, it leads to chronic limb threatening ischaemia (CLTI). Over half of those with
symptomatic PAD will die, have an amputation or cardiovascular event within five years of diagnosis. CTLI
prognosis is extremely poor; commensurate with terminal cancer. The economic burden of PAD is substantial and
rising.
PAD patients report dissatisfaction with care and improving outcomes across the PAD symptom range is the
highest ranking James Lind Alliance research priority among all vascular conditions. Providing PAD rehabilitation
and supporting self-management will save money. Yet, equitable access to community rehabilitation is
compromised by lack of acceptable, feasible interventions that were co-designed with patients and providers,
restricted resources and a high and increasing demand from a diverse patient group with complex needs. Many
people with PAD are also excluded from research and unable to access or use new treatments e.g., digital
interventions.
New scalable, co-designed, inclusive PAD interventions such as provider supported community rehabilitation, self-
management programmes and ‘next-generation’ mobile based health technologies (mhealth) are promising. Yet,
evidence is mixed, none have been designed with PAD patients and providers, or commissioned in the NHS.
‑
The aim of this professorship is to transform the care for people with peripheral artery disease through community
rehabilitation interventions. Over five work packages, I will lead research that will:
1. Maximise diverse and inclusive involvement, participation and engagement of PAD underserved groups.
2a. Through systematic review, qualitative and participatory approaches, codesign an inclusive supported self-
management intervention to improve quality of life in CLTI patients.
2b. Develop trial protocol, funding application, set-up/commence a trial.
3a. Through secondary analysis of existing quantitative and qualitative data, participatory approaches, co-refine
and feasibility test an inclusive home-based walking exercise behaviour-change programme for people with IC in
primary and community services.
3b. Develop trial protocol, funding application, set-up/commence a trial.
4a. Through qualitative approaches, co-design, adaptation and user testing, develop an inclusive mhealth
intervention for PAD patients to increase physical activity.
4b. Develop trial protocol and submit funding application.
5. Drawing on data from workstreams 2-4, specify the implementation context, outcomes and mechanisms and co-
design a programme theory, preliminary implementation and evaluation strategies for inclusive PAD community
rehabilitation services.
This ambitious programme of work balances building on my existing work with guaranteed high impact outputs,
and projects which promise a rapid upward trajectory within five years. It will spearhead the shift of PAD care into
the community and develop research capacity in under represented disciplines.
Mitigating research and digital exclusion and involving patients and the public are central to this research. Patients
identified the research questions, helped plan this research and will help deliver and mobilise research progress
and findings using co-designed, creative approaches.

overall_ppi.text:
research with diverse PAD patients and their carers in coproduction meetings that have shaped the focus,
research questions, and plan. Nineteen people with lived experience of PAD, including people from Black, African
Caribbean, South Asian, and Eastern European communities and those with CLTI shared their experiences of
living with PAD and their treatment. To reach people from underserved communities who did not want to join a
group or those experiencing digital exclusion, I talked to them individually via telephone or in-person.
They discussed the barriers to receiving rehabilitation in hospital (e.g. time, travel) and how they wanted to receive
co-ordinated and responsive rehabilitation/support locally. They described how this research was ‘much needed’
as this part of NHS care was ‘unacceptable and burdensome’ and they asked for ‘better access’ to rehabilitation
and ways to help them look after their condition and symptoms independently.
Patients with CLTI described having treatment in hospital and then ‘being left in a vacuum at home’. They
welcomed sessions with a provider to help them ‘make the most of their lives’. People with IC told me that home-
based walking exercise was often ‘overlooked as a treatment’, but that specific walking advice with support was
needed. They liked the content and format of MOSAIC1 and commented that having this intervention provided in
the community with ‘enough time with the therapist’ was key to helping them walk more. They didn’t mind who
treated them ‘just as long as they were well trained and supervised’.
All advisors with lived experience of PAD recognised that becoming more active was important and wanted to
explore mhealth using activity trackers, although some were nervous about this being the only treatment option.
They were worried about ‘everything going online’ and suggested developing an intervention that was easy to use,
inclusive and accessible to everyone. This advice changed the focus of this workstream. The public advisors
helped identify research questions and develop the research plan. Three advisors (two from ethnic communities)
read the public summary.
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
My public involvement strategy aligns with the NIHR 2025-2030 strategic commitments and six UK Public
consistency99,100.
Involvement Standards, ensuring valuable, sustainable relationships, quality and
Standards 1-3: Our advisory group includes men, women and members of underrepresented populations (n=9). I
will seek further representation from underserved populations and diverse geographical locations by continuing to
work with patient and community groups. I will employ a dedicated research inclusion and public involvement
officer to plan, support and liaise with our advisors and community groups, co-ordinate our regular meetings and
ad hoc activities. I will be responsible for oversight, planning and reporting of involvement, impact, and diversity in
involvement, participation and engagement. Research inclusion and cultural competency training will be offered to
public advisors.
Standards 4-6: My team will meet with our public advisors every 6-months to discuss workstream progress, and
work with them in between on focused activities. We will balance meaningful involvement with the risk of over-
burdening them. Digital exclusion will be mitigated by providing iPads, data packages and technical support, if
needed, or if a member is not able to join the larger online meeting, they will be sent materials and can speak
individually either remotely or via telephone. Advisors will be offered involvement in elements of workstreams that
particularly interest them and invited to monthly research management meetings. Planned activities include
providing feedback on public facing materials such as websites, reviewing study processes to enhance
acceptability and experience of participation, promoting public awareness of the study, reaching hidden
communities and dissemination. We will fund our public advisors to co-present at conferences and become co-
investigators on funding applications.
Standard 6: Public involvement will be monitored, reviewed and reported at research management meetings, with
public advisors providing feedback, reviewing and agreeing reported impact. This will enable reflection on our
processes to improve advisor experience and explore quality and impact. We will report our public involvement in
research using the Guidance for Reporting Involvement of Patients and the Public 2-short-form checklist79. All
involvement activity and expenses will be reimbursed in line with NIHR policy.

public in your proposed research, please justify why
